definition: Objective finding that can be observed or measured
rationale: A sign is an objective finding observed by the examiner such as vital signs skin color swelling or bleeding. Signs can be measured or observed unlike symptoms which are subjective.

definition: Subjective experience reported by the patient
rationale: A symptom is a subjective experience reported by the patient such as pain nausea dizziness or weakness. Symptoms cannot be directly observed or measured by the examiner.

definition: Sudden onset or short duration
rationale: Acute refers to conditions with sudden onset or short duration typically days to weeks. Acute MI acute appendicitis and acute asthma attack are examples of sudden-onset conditions.

definition: Long-lasting or recurring condition
rationale: Chronic refers to conditions persisting over long periods typically months to years. COPD chronic heart failure and diabetes are chronic conditions that may have acute exacerbations.

definition: Affecting both sides
rationale: Bilateral means affecting both sides of the body. Bilateral leg swelling bilateral wheezing or bilateral pupils being equal are examples of bilateral findings.

definition: Affecting one side only
rationale: Unilateral means affecting only one side. Unilateral weakness stroke unilateral pupil dilation herniation and unilateral absent breath sounds pneumothorax are significant unilateral findings.

definition: Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath
rationale: Dyspnea is the subjective sensation of difficulty breathing or air hunger. It may occur at rest or with exertion. The degree of dyspnea helps assess severity of respiratory conditions.

definition: Difficulty breathing when lying flat
rationale: Orthopnea is dyspnea that occurs or worsens when lying flat and improves when sitting up. It is characteristic of heart failure due to increased venous return when supine.

definition: Abnormally rapid respiratory rate
rationale: Tachypnea is respiratory rate above normal typically over 20 in adults. It may indicate respiratory distress hypoxia metabolic acidosis fever anxiety or pain.

definition: Abnormally slow respiratory rate
rationale: Bradypnea is respiratory rate below normal typically under 12 in adults. Causes include CNS depression from drugs or head injury hypothermia and impending respiratory failure.

definition: Absence of breathing
rationale: Apnea is complete cessation of breathing. Brief apnea occurs normally during sleep. Prolonged apnea requires immediate ventilatory support as it quickly leads to death.

definition: Increased depth of breathing
rationale: Hyperpnea is increased depth of breathing not necessarily increased rate. It may be normal during exercise or pathological in conditions like metabolic acidosis Kussmaul respirations.

definition: Visible pulling in of tissues during inspiration
rationale: Retractions are visible indrawing of tissues intercostal supraclavicular substernal during inspiration indicating increased work of breathing. They suggest significant respiratory distress.

definition: Widening of nostrils during breathing
rationale: Nasal flaring is widening of the nostrils with inspiration. It is especially significant in infants and children as a sign of respiratory distress indicating increased effort to breathe.

definition: Sitting upright leaning forward on hands
rationale: Tripod position is sitting upright leaning forward with hands on knees or surface. Patients instinctively adopt this position to maximize breathing capacity. It indicates significant respiratory distress.

definition: Sensation of rapid pounding or fluttering heartbeat
rationale: Palpitations are the subjective sensation of abnormal heartbeat often described as racing pounding skipping or fluttering. They may indicate arrhythmia anxiety or be benign.

definition: Fainting or temporary loss of consciousness
rationale: Syncope is transient loss of consciousness from temporary decreased brain blood flow. Causes range from benign vasovagal to serious cardiac arrhythmias. Recovery is usually rapid and complete.

definition: Feeling of almost fainting without loss of consciousness
rationale: Presyncope is the feeling of impending faint with symptoms like lightheadedness weakness tunnel vision and nausea without actually losing consciousness. It suggests the same underlying causes as syncope.

definition: Swelling from fluid accumulation in tissues
rationale: Edema is abnormal fluid accumulation in tissues causing swelling. Peripheral edema in legs suggests right heart failure or venous insufficiency. Pulmonary edema indicates left heart failure.

definition: Edema that retains indentation when pressed
rationale: Pitting edema leaves a temporary indentation when pressed with a finger. The depth and duration of the pit indicates severity. It commonly occurs in dependent areas like ankles and feet.

definition: Abnormal sensation like tingling or numbness
rationale: Paresthesia is abnormal sensation including tingling burning numbness or pins and needles. It may indicate nerve compression stroke diabetic neuropathy or other neurological conditions.

definition: Complete loss of muscle function
rationale: Paralysis is complete inability to move affected muscles. Hemiplegia affects one side. Paraplegia affects lower body. Quadriplegia affects all four extremities. Causes include stroke and spinal cord injury.

definition: Weakness or partial paralysis
rationale: Paresis is muscle weakness or partial loss of movement less severe than complete paralysis. Hemiparesis one-sided weakness is a common stroke finding.

definition: Uncoordinated movement and balance problems
rationale: Ataxia is lack of muscle coordination affecting gait balance and speech. It may indicate cerebellar dysfunction alcohol intoxication stroke or other neurological problems.

definition: Difficulty with speech or language
rationale: Aphasia is impaired ability to produce or understand language due to brain damage typically from stroke. Expressive aphasia affects speech production. Receptive aphasia affects comprehension.

definition: Slurred or difficult speech from muscle weakness
rationale: Dysarthria is slurred or difficult speech from weakness of muscles controlling speech. Unlike aphasia the patient knows what they want to say but cannot articulate clearly. Common in stroke.

definition: Abnormal electrical activity in brain causing involuntary movements
rationale: Seizures result from abnormal electrical discharge in the brain causing varied manifestations from brief staring spells to generalized convulsions depending on location and spread of activity.

definition: Warning sensation before seizure or migraine
rationale: An aura is a sensory warning that precedes a seizure or migraine. It may be visual auditory olfactory or emotional. Patients who recognize their aura can sometimes get to safety before a seizure.

definition: Sensation of needing to vomit
rationale: Nausea is the subjective sensation of impending vomiting or stomach upset. It may occur with or without actual vomiting and has many causes including GI problems medications and motion.

definition: Forceful expulsion of stomach contents
rationale: Vomiting or emesis is forceful expulsion of gastric contents through the mouth. It may be caused by GI problems toxins increased intracranial pressure pregnancy or medications.

definition: Vomiting blood
rationale: Hematemesis is vomiting blood which may be bright red fresh bleeding or coffee-ground appearing digested blood. It indicates upper GI bleeding from ulcers varices or other sources.

definition: Black tarry stool from upper GI bleeding
rationale: Melena is black tarry foul-smelling stool resulting from digested blood typically from upper GI bleeding. The dark color comes from blood being digested as it passes through the GI tract.

definition: Bright red blood in stool
rationale: Hematochezia is passage of bright red blood through the rectum usually indicating lower GI bleeding from hemorrhoids diverticulosis or colorectal pathology. Massive upper GI bleeding can also cause it.

definition: Abnormal enlargement or swelling
rationale: Distension refers to abnormal enlargement typically of the abdomen. Abdominal distension may indicate bowel obstruction ascites internal bleeding or gastric dilation from positive pressure ventilation.

definition: Voluntary muscle tensing to protect painful area
rationale: Guarding is voluntary tensing of abdominal muscles when the examiner palpates a painful area. It indicates underlying inflammation or injury. Voluntary guarding can be overcome with distraction.

definition: Involuntary constant abdominal muscle tension
rationale: Rigidity or involuntary guarding is constant abdominal muscle tension that cannot be overcome. It indicates peritonitis from ruptured viscus or other serious intra-abdominal pathology.

definition: Pain that spreads from one area to another
rationale: Radiating pain spreads from its origin along a nerve pathway. Cardiac pain radiating to the arm or jaw and sciatica radiating down the leg are examples of radiating pain.

definition: Pain felt in location distant from its source
rationale: Referred pain is felt at a location different from the pathology causing it. Examples include shoulder pain from diaphragm irritation Kehr sign and jaw pain from cardiac ischemia.

definition: Cramping pain that comes and goes in waves
rationale: Colicky or cramping pain waxes and wanes in intensity typically from hollow organ obstruction or spasm. Kidney stones gallstones and bowel obstruction cause characteristic colicky pain.

definition: Redness of skin from increased blood flow
rationale: Flushing is redness of the skin caused by vasodilation and increased blood flow. It may occur with fever allergic reaction emotional response or certain medications.

definition: Patchy discoloration of skin from poor perfusion
rationale: Mottling is irregular patchy skin discoloration typically indicating poor peripheral perfusion. In shock the skin may appear marbled or blotchy especially on the extremities and trunk.

definition: Tiny pinpoint red spots from broken capillaries
rationale: Petechiae are pinpoint non-blanching red spots caused by bleeding from capillaries into skin. They may indicate low platelets clotting disorders or severe infection like meningococcemia.

definition: Purple spots from bleeding under the skin larger than petechiae
rationale: Purpura are purple discolorations larger than petechiae 3-10mm from bleeding under the skin. Like petechiae they do not blanch with pressure and indicate bleeding disorders or vasculitis.

definition: Bruise from bleeding under the skin
rationale: Ecchymosis is a bruise larger than 1cm caused by blood leaking from vessels into tissue. It changes color over time as hemoglobin breaks down from purple to green to yellow as it heals.

definition: Hives from allergic reaction
rationale: Urticaria or hives are raised itchy wheals on the skin from histamine release in allergic reactions. They can appear anywhere blanch with pressure and may be localized or widespread.

definition: Itching sensation
rationale: Pruritus is the sensation of itching that creates an urge to scratch. It commonly accompanies allergic reactions skin conditions and some systemic diseases.

definition: General feeling of discomfort illness or uneasiness
rationale: Malaise is a vague feeling of bodily discomfort or uneasiness often accompanying the onset of illness. Patients may say they just do not feel right without specific complaints.

definition: Abnormal drowsiness or lack of energy
rationale: Lethargy is abnormal drowsiness sluggishness or lack of energy. Patients are arousable but difficult to keep awake. It may indicate infection metabolic problems medications or neurological issues.

definition: Decreased alertness with slow response to stimulation
rationale: Obtundation is a moderately decreased level of consciousness with slowed responses to stimulation. Patients can be aroused but are not fully alert. It falls between lethargy and stupor on the consciousness spectrum.

definition: Near unconsciousness requiring strong stimulation to arouse
rationale: Stupor is a state of near unconsciousness where only vigorous or painful stimulation produces a response. It indicates severe CNS depression and is more serious than lethargy or obtundation.
